ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general congee/rice porridge class; no specific Batang fish porridge entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM covers rice porridge/congee as a category. No specific fish porridge sub-code exists; closest defensible code applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Batang fish porridge or Chinese fish congee. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Batang Fish Gourmet Porridge weighs approximately 500 g, reflecting a typical single-serve restaurant or hawker portion in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food (Fish Porridge / Congee with Fish)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for fish porridge and congee-type dishes. Values for Batang Fish Gourmet Porridge are adapted from HPB fish porridge entries and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) data for Spanish mackerel and white rice congee components. The input record values (energy 76 kcal, protein 7 g, fat 2.4 g, carbohydrate 6.4 g per 100 g) are consistent with HPB fish porridge reference ranges and are retained. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where HPB and USDA do not report disaggregated values for this composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Rice congee / rice porridge analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Batang Fish Gourmet Porridge. GI of 78 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice congee / rice porridge (GI ~78, glucose reference), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The high GI reflects starch gelatinisation during prolonged boiling. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (78 × 32) / 100 = 24.96; however, because the dish is highly diluted with water and the carbohydrate density per 100 g is only 6.4 g, the effective GL per 500 g serving = (78 × 32) / 100 = 24.96. Corrected GL per serving = (78 × (6.4 × 5)) / 100 = (78 × 32) / 100 = 24.96. Note: GL per serving reported as 24.96 rounded to 25.0 for the 500 g bowl. Revised: GL = GI × net carbs per serving (g) / 100 = 78 × 32 / 100 = 24.96 ≈ 25.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 240 mg per 100 g (1200 mg per 500 g bowl) approaches the upper daily threshold for hypertensive individuals; preserved vegetables (chai poh / zha cai) are a significant sodium contributor.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced or omitted preserved vegetables, avoid adding soy sauce or salt at the table, and limit portion size to half a bowl to keep sodium intake within a single-meal target of 600–800 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice porridge (congee) has a high glycemic index (~78) due to starch gelatinisation during prolonged boiling, despite the low carbohydrate density of 6.4 g per 100 g; a 500 g bowl delivers approximately 32 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~25.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor post-meal blood glucose after consuming porridge, limit portion to 250–300 g, pair with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, and avoid adding fried dough fritters (you tiao) which substantially raise the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Batang fish (Spanish mackerel) is a rich source of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which have established cardioprotective effects; saturated fat is low at 0.8 g per 100 g and trans fat is absent.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is broadly suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its omega-3 content from Batang fish and low saturated fat profile; however, sodium at 240 mg per 100 g warrants caution — request no preserved vegetables and no added salt to keep the dish heart-friendly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Batang fish provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 EPA/DHA; ginger contributes gingerols with documented anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is very low and trans fat is absent.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; request omission of preserved vegetables to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling, and ensure Batang fish remains the primary protein to maximise omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Batang Fish Gourmet Porridge (马鲛鱼粥, Bubur Ikan Batang) is a Chinese-Singaporean comfort dish consisting of a smooth, slow-simmered rice congee base enriched with tender slices of Batang fish (Spanish mackerel, approximately 130 g per serving), minced pork, fresh ginger, and preserved vegetables such as chai poh or zha cai. The dish is served in a 500 g bowl and prepared by prolonged boiling of white rice until the grains fully break down into a silky, thick porridge. The texture is soft and creamy with a savoury, mildly briny broth. Batang fish contributes a firm, slightly oily flesh with a clean oceanic flavour and is a meaningful source of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), high-quality protein (7 g per 100 g), and B vitamins. The dish provides 76 kcal per 100 g, making it relatively low in energy density due to its high water content. Sodium is moderate at 240 mg per 100 g (1200 mg per 500 g bowl), primarily from the broth and preserved vegetables. The glycemic index is high (~78) due to starch gelatinisation during prolonged cooking, though the glycemic load per serving is approximately 25 given the diluted carbohydrate density. Clinically, this dish is broadly suitable for cardiovascular health due to its omega-3 content and low saturated fat, but requires sodium management for hypertensive individuals and portion control for those with Type 2 Diabetes. It is a popular hawker and restaurant dish in Singapore, commonly consumed at breakfast or as a light meal, and is culturally associated with Chinese-Singaporean culinary traditions.

How much protein is in fish porridge?
What are the ingredients in batang fish porridge?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo fish porridge reference ranges and are supported by cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central Spanish mackerel and rice congee data. However, no exact HPB entry for Batang Fish Gourmet Porridge exists; values are adapted from the closest HPB fish porridge category entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database rice congee entry. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: disaggregated fatty acid data for this composite fish porridge dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium is not reported for this composite dish in HPB Foodo; an estimated value of ~200 mg/100 g was noted in the input record but is not confirmed by an approved source and is therefore excluded from per_100g. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not explicitly reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for the exact preparation. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported for this composite dish in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2024. 5. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per input record and consistent with HPB fish porridge data — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). 6. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains minced pork; it is not suitable for Muslim consumers unless a pork-free version is requested. 7. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database rice congee entry (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Batang Fish Gourmet Porridge exists in the approved GI database. 8. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (78 × 32) / 100 = 24.96, reported as 25.0.
